title: Thanksgiving

jokes.text: Are they gonna hit the minibar tonight at their hotel?
jokes.setup: An aide asks Selina for fun details about the turkeys to give the press
jokes.punchline: Selina's first instinct is to anthropomorphize the turkeys as rowdy hotel guests hitting the minibar — a non-sequitur that reveals her mind works in strange, PR-spin directions
jokes.explanation: A quick absurdist riff that feels organic to Selina — she's constantly trying to spin everything into a fun story and this is her mind at work in a goofy, undignified way. The economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Well, they were bred to be eaten, so they'll probably collapse from their own weight and die in a year or two, tops. Is there a more fun way to say that?
jokes.setup: Selina asks for fun facts about the pardoned turkeys going to a petting zoo
jokes.punchline: The aide bluntly states the turkeys will die soon anyway due to being bred for consumption, then Selina's follow-up 'Is there a more fun way to say that?' is the actual punchline — treating horrifying truth as a mere PR problem
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely sharp joke. The setup is the cheerful turkey pardon ceremony; the punchline is biological reality delivered flatly. But the real genius is Selina's response — she doesn't dispute the truth, she just wants it repackaged. It's a perfect encapsulation of political spin culture and Selina's character in one line. Strong structure and economy.

jokes.text: I hereby declare Drumstick and Cranberry pardoned from the Thanksgiving dinner table — and also for tax evasion.
jokes.setup: Selina delivers the formal, ceremonial turkey pardon proclamation in dignified presidential tones
jokes.punchline: She appends 'and also for tax evasion' — an absurd escalation that breaks the solemnity and suggests she's winging it for laughs
jokes.explanation: A competent absurdist add-on to the pardon ceremony. The 'tax evasion' escalation is a reliable format (unexpected crime tacked onto formal speech) but Iannucci executes it cleanly. Not groundbreaking but well-placed and gets a genuine laugh.

jokes.text: Someone's got a case of the blue badge blue balls.
jokes.setup: Dan has been issued a green security badge instead of blue, causing him to be stopped; Ben explains it's a security office screwup
jokes.punchline: Ben coins 'blue badge blue balls' — a crude but efficient portmanteau insult for Dan's frustrated status anxiety
jokes.explanation: A neat bit of wordplay — the alliterative rhyme of 'blue badge blue balls' is satisfying and crude in the Veep tradition. Ben delivers it with perfect deflating casualness. Good economy.

jokes.text: Missouri's the largest turkey-producing state in the union. Second only to Minnesota followed by North Carolina. Ma'am. And Arkansas.
jokes.setup: Selina makes a quip about using the turkey pardon to leverage Missouri's congressional delegation
jokes.punchline: Kent responds with a rapid-fire series of turkey production rankings delivered with complete earnestness, including the deadpan afterthought 'And Arkansas' — a perfect Kent data-dump non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: This is pure Kent character comedy. The delayed 'Ma'am' beat between North Carolina and Arkansas suggests he briefly stops himself before adding a fourth state — as if he's trying to restrain his encyclopedic compulsion. The 'And Arkansas' afterthought is the kind of tiny precision that makes Veep writing exceptional. High character integration score because this could ONLY be Kent.

jokes.text: Maybe I can pardon a car with a defective airbag and we can wrap up Michigan.
jokes.setup: Selina is pleased with the turkey pardon as a political tool to win votes
jokes.punchline: She riffs on the concept by suggesting pardoning inanimate objects with product-recall issues to win other states — absurd escalation that reveals how transactional she finds everything
jokes.explanation: Selina's absurdist logic is on good form here — she applies the pardoning framework so literally and transactionally that it reveals the hollowness of the ceremony. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Michigan is actually the largest producer of battery acid.
jokes.setup: Selina says pardoning a defective car could wrap up Michigan
jokes.punchline: Kent immediately fact-checks with a completely irrelevant piece of data (battery acid production) delivered as if it's helpful
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent Kent joke. The non-sequitur fact-check is so disconnected from the conversation (battery acid has nothing to do with the airbag joke) that it reveals Kent's mind operates on its own parallel track. The deadpan delivery makes it funnier. High originality because the specific detail 'battery acid' is wonderfully random and specific.

jokes.text: Can somebody reach around the back of Kent's head and power him down?
jokes.setup: Kent has delivered yet another unsolicited data point
jokes.punchline: Selina describes Kent as a malfunctioning machine you need to physically switch off — dehumanizing him in the most specific, mechanical way possible
jokes.explanation: 'Reach around the back of his head and power him down' is a vivid, specific image that perfectly captures Kent's robotic affect. The 'reach around' phrasing also carries a mild inadvertent dirty undertone that sharpens the laugh. Well-constructed and quotable.

jokes.text: My eyelids are seriously starting to look like Keith Richards' ball sack.
jokes.setup: Selina is preparing for the day, lamenting how the election has worn her out
jokes.punchline: She compares her under-eye bags to Keith Richards' scrotum — an unexpectedly graphic and specific celebrity comparison that captures both her vanity and her unfiltered way of speaking
jokes.explanation: A highly quotable line. 'Keith Richards' ball sack' is specific enough to be vivid but universal enough (everyone knows what Keith Richards looks like) to land immediately. The vulgarity is calibrated — shocking but not gratuitous. High quotability because this is the kind of line people repeat.

jokes.text: Oh, please. He wishes.
jokes.setup: Selina says her eyelids look like Keith Richards' ball sack
jokes.punchline: Ben responds 'Oh, please. He wishes.' — implying Selina's eyelids are in worse shape than Keith Richards' scrotum, which is both an insult and an absurd escalation delivered with complete deadpan
jokes.explanation: Three words that escalate joke 10 perfectly. 'He wishes' implies Selina's eyelids are in WORSE condition than Richards' famously ruined skin — which is simultaneously an insult to Selina and an absurdist compliment to Richards. The economy score is near-perfect: maximum payload, minimum words. This is the kind of callback/escalation that makes Veep's walk-and-talk dialogue sing.

jokes.text: Husband? You're married? / Yes. Last year. / 250 people, no one from work.
jokes.setup: Selina is chatting with Marjorie and discovers she's married
jokes.punchline: Marjorie's '250 people, no one from work' — a pre-emptive answer to the next logical question (why wasn't Selina invited?) that is bluntly delivered as a complete explanation requiring no apology
jokes.explanation: This is an outstanding character comedy moment. '250 people, no one from work' is one of the episode's best lines — it's perfectly economical, it answers a question that wasn't asked, and it tells us that Marjorie is so compartmentalized that her entire professional life (including the President of the United States) was deliberately excluded from her wedding. Selina's stunned reaction ('Husband? You're married?') followed by this flat explanation is a great exchange. High economy and character integration.

jokes.text: God, all that exercising and Glorious Leader still can't seem to take off a pound, right? That haircut's not doing him any favors.
jokes.setup: An aide reports that North Korea is conducting a military exercise
jokes.punchline: Selina pivots from geopolitical concern to personal appearance critique of Kim Jong-un — the banality of her vanity applied to an authoritarian dictator
jokes.explanation: Competent character comedy — Selina's shallowness applied to geopolitics is a reliable Veep gag format. The 'Glorious Leader' wordplay with 'exercising' is a decent pivot. But it's a slightly easy target and the haircut observation, while true to character, is the kind of joke anyone could make.

jokes.text: My rusty sheriff's badge? / Oh, I'm sure that showing your anus gets you into all your regular social clubs, but this is the White House mess.
jokes.setup: Jonah demands to see Dan's badge to verify he belongs in the White House mess
jokes.punchline: Dan says 'my rusty sheriff's badge' (slang for anus/sphincter); Jonah takes the opportunity to lecture Dan about using his anus as access credentials in other venues while enforcing White House rules
jokes.explanation: The 'rusty sheriff's badge' slang setup with Jonah's formal elaboration on anus-based social access is a good comedy exchange. Jonah gets to be both a bureaucratic petty tyrant and inadvertently crude simultaneously. The image of Dan's 'regular social clubs' where anus-showing grants access is absurdist and funny.

jokes.text: I hope you die a horrible death choking on a red, glistening dog dick. / Have a lovely Thanksgiving, Mrs. Ryan.
jokes.setup: Dan is furious at Jonah for enforcing the badge rules; Jonah calls security
jokes.punchline: Dan delivers an extreme curse, then immediately pivots to warmly bidding farewell to Jonah's mother who has just appeared — the whiplash from extreme profanity/violence to tender politeness is the joke
jokes.explanation: The structure is excellent — the curse ('red, glistening dog dick') is vivid and extreme enough to be funny on its own, but the immediate pivot to 'Have a lovely Thanksgiving, Mrs. Ryan' is the real joke. The 'red, glistening' adjectives on the dog dick are the kind of specific escalation Veep specializes in. The social whiplash is perfectly timed and the maternal address lands the scene. One of the episode's most quotable moments.

jokes.text: I invited Richard to come back with us for Thanksgiving tomorrow. / Mom, you don't invite my friends to Thanksgiving dinner. I invite my friends to Thanksgiving dinner. / Richard, would you like to come to Thanksgiving dinner?
jokes.setup: Richard has been invited to Thanksgiving by someone who is clearly not in a position to issue the invitation
jokes.punchline: The territorial protest about who invites whom to Thanksgiving, immediately followed by the inviter simply re-issuing the invitation directly to Richard anyway
jokes.explanation: A functional family-dynamics joke — the territorial claim over Thanksgiving invitations ends with the same invitation being issued anyway. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: A De-bagging? Is that what you just said? I thought you said tea-bagging. No, no, no. That's my department.
jokes.setup: The doctor explains the eyelid procedure called a 'de-bagging' in clinical terms
jokes.punchline: Selina and Gary both mishear it as 'tea-bagging'; Gary eagerly claims it as his department — which is both a sex joke and a darkly funny commentary on Gary's servile devotion to Selina
jokes.explanation: The wordplay is the entry point but the real joke is Gary immediately claiming tea-bagging as 'his department' — which is disturbing in its implications about his relationship with Selina and played with perfect earnestness. High character integration because only Gary would say this with no awareness of how it sounds.

jokes.text: He means that literally. He doesn't know what the other thing is.
jokes.setup: Gary has said 'that's my department' regarding tea-bagging
jokes.punchline: Selina clarifies to the doctor that Gary is completely innocent of the sexual meaning — both defending and diminishing Gary simultaneously
jokes.explanation: A strong follow-through on the tea-bagging joke. Selina's explanation is efficient and characterful — it captures the weird mother-child dynamic she has with Gary while keeping the joke moving. 'He doesn't know what the other thing is' lands particularly well.

jokes.text: That would make you a baby.
jokes.setup: The doctor assures Selina she'll look ten years younger after the procedure
jokes.punchline: Gary immediately does the math and concludes she'd be a baby — played completely straight, with no self-awareness that this is insulting
jokes.explanation: Four words, perfectly timed. Gary's literal-minded interpretation accidentally insults Selina (calling her old) while trying to compliment her. The joke works because Gary is completely sincere — he's done the math and is sharing it helpfully. Pure character comedy at its most economical.

jokes.text: POTUS is still getting permanently Photoshopped. Let's get the head of the CDC out there, calm everyone down. Let's send Tom James out as well. He's a tall drink of Xanax.
jokes.setup: Selina is in her eye surgery, incommunicado, and there's a salmonella outbreak developing
jokes.punchline: 'Permanently Photoshopped' is a sharp euphemism for cosmetic surgery; 'tall drink of Xanax' is a brilliant original metaphor for Tom James' calming establishment demeanor
jokes.explanation: 'Tall drink of Xanax' is the standout line of this section — it's genuinely original, perfectly economical, and captures Tom James' entire character in four words. The 'permanently Photoshopped' euphemism is also excellent. Ben's lines here demonstrate the show's signature ability to pack character insights and plot information into a single memorable phrase. This approaches the 8.0 anchor quality.

jokes.text: The number of people taken ill is orders of magnitude below statistical significance. Do people not understand basic nonparametric statistics? Or how to cook a fucking turkey.
jokes.setup: The team is responding to the growing salmonella outbreak
jokes.punchline: Kent frames a public health crisis through the lens of statistical pedantry, then punctures his own pomposity with the crude 'or how to cook a fucking turkey' — a rare moment of Kent's practical frustration breaking through
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best character moments. Kent invoking 'nonparametric statistics' in a public health context is the pompous data-head we know, but the pivot to 'or how to cook a fucking turkey' — delivered in the same register — is a perfect structural twist. The profanity lands extra hard precisely because Kent is usually so controlled. High character integration: only Kent frames a salmonella outbreak as a failure of statistical literacy.

jokes.text: The number of the beast Sidney Purcell. I don't know why he keeps calling me. His comprehension of the word no hovers somewhere around drunk college quarterback level.
jokes.setup: Tom James sees a call from Sidney Purcell and is annoyed
jokes.punchline: 'Drunk college quarterback level' as the metric for comprehending 'no' — a fresh, specific simile that evokes both privilege and selective hearing with comic precision
jokes.explanation: 'Drunk college quarterback' is a vivid specific archetype — immediately recognizable, fresh as a simile. The 'number of the beast' introduction for Sidney is also a nice touch. Tom James establishing himself with a sharp line is good character work.

jokes.text: Oh, this is gonna wipe that crooked smile off Jonah Ryan's face. I think that's genetic.
jokes.setup: Dan receives something from Tom James meant to disadvantage Jonah Ryan
jokes.punchline: Tom James deadpans 'I think that's genetic' about Jonah's crooked smile — a clinical, detached way to insult Jonah that implies his deformity is inherent and thus extra pathetic
jokes.explanation: The deadpan delivery of 'I think that's genetic' as a matter-of-fact medical observation rather than an insult is what makes this work. Tom James' patrician condescension applied to Jonah works well.

jokes.text: I try not to act like an alcoholic father who has just stepped on a Lego.
jokes.setup: Tom James is establishing his management style to Dan, contrasting himself with Selina's chaotic leadership
jokes.punchline: 'An alcoholic father who has just stepped on a Lego' — the combination of the chronic dysfunction (alcoholism) with the acute domestic trigger (Lego) creates a hyper-specific comic image for explosive, unpredictable rage
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's strongest lines. 'Alcoholic father who stepped on a Lego' is a genuinely original simile — the Lego detail transforms a generic 'bad boss' insult into something specific, vivid, and universally recognizable. The Lego is a stroke of genius: everyone knows the Lego-stepping pain, which makes the alcoholic father's disproportionate rage both funny and unsettling. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor example in terms of economy and specificity.

jokes.text: Raise your right hand and repeat the oath. [Dan raises hand] I'm fucking with you. Come on, let's go.
jokes.setup: Tom James solemnly tells Dan to raise his hand and repeat an oath, implying a serious initiation
jokes.punchline: 'I'm fucking with you' — a deliberate misdirection that also reveals Tom James' controlled playfulness and Dan's eagerness/gullibility
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection — we and Dan both fall for the setup. It's effective because it reveals Tom James is not the solemn establishment figure he appears to be. The reaction beat of Dan raising his hand earnestly (inferred from Dan saying 'That's very good, sir') adds to the comedy.

jokes.text: No, Wendy's pissed. I left her at home with her folks and my folks plus her kids. Shit, I don't even know who's at my house. I don't even know if they're in town. I hate to admit it, but sometimes I'm scared of Wendy. She still has a great rack, though.
jokes.setup: Mike says the salmonella press conference is fine because everyone's watching football
jokes.punchline: Mike spirals into a confession about his home life — fear of his wife — before undercutting it with the irrelevant observation about her breasts, revealing the jumbled, survival-mode thinking of a man in domestic chaos
jokes.explanation: The escalating confession structure works well — Mike is spiraling through genuine anxiety, then the 'great rack' non-sequitur deflects all the anxiety with crass male coping. It's character-specific: Mike is the show's most haplessly ordinary person and this captures his muddled domestic life perfectly.

jokes.text: Jesus, you look like a southern housewife who just burned her husband's dinner.
jokes.setup: Selina emerges from eye surgery with significant bruising and swelling, fishing for compliments
jokes.punchline: Ben's assessment is blunt, specific, and devastating — 'southern housewife who just burned her husband's dinner' implies domestically-inflicted facial injuries (domestic abuse) while maintaining plausible deniability
jokes.explanation: A dark and specific insult. 'Southern housewife who just burned her husband's dinner' is a vivid class and regional image that implies domestic violence with the precision the show favors. Ben's delivery of brutal truths as casual observations is peak Veep.

jokes.text: No one should see you while your face is... marinating.
jokes.setup: The team debates whether Selina should make a public statement about the salmonella outbreak despite her bruised post-surgery face
jokes.punchline: 'Marinating' as the euphemism for a bruised, swollen, post-surgical face — and with Thanksgiving's food context running throughout the episode, the word lands with extra darkly funny resonance
jokes.explanation: 'Marinating' is a brilliantly original euphemism — it's crude, it's culinary (perfectly themed to a Thanksgiving episode), and it captures the bloody, fluid nature of bruising in a way that's disgusting and funny simultaneously. This is the kind of precise word choice that elevates Veep above standard political comedy. One of the episode's best single words.

jokes.text: Okay, she just had 10 years taken off her eyes. Well, I don't think they left yet.
jokes.setup: Gary defends Selina's appearance post-surgery by saying she had 10 years taken off
jokes.punchline: Karen suggests the years haven't 'left yet' — implying the surgery result isn't visible yet and she looks worse
jokes.explanation: A decent visual joke about the post-surgery swelling. The construction is competent — 'the years haven't left yet' is a nice way to say she looks terrible without saying it.

jokes.text: Well, I have a bachelor's degree in hotel management from Cornell University. No, you don't.
jokes.setup: Gary protests being dismissed by the doctor, citing his qualifications
jokes.punchline: Selina flatly denies his claimed Cornell degree — not questioning it, but flatly negating it as if she would know, which implies she does know, which makes it both funnier and darker
jokes.explanation: 'No, you don't' is delivered with such certainty that it implies either Gary is lying about the degree or Selina simply refuses to accept any Gary credential. The brevity is the key — it's a flat negation with no elaboration, which is the funniest possible response. The economy score is near-perfect. High character integration: only Selina would deny Gary's resume with this kind of casual authority.

jokes.text: No! He's the old face of this administration. This is the new face.
jokes.setup: The team suggests having VP Doyle make a statement while Selina's face is recovering
jokes.punchline: Selina insists on herself as 'the new face' while her face is literally bruised and grotesque — the dramatic irony is that her 'new face' is objectively worse in this moment
jokes.explanation: The visual irony of insisting you're the new face while looking terrible is a strong comedic concept. The line also works thematically — Selina's vanity-driven surgery is literally making her look worse in the short term while she tries to maintain control. Character-integrated irony.

jokes.text: It really hurts to roll my eyes. Okay, yes, ask Doyle.
jokes.setup: Selina reluctantly agrees to let Doyle make the statement, and tries to express displeasure with an eye-roll
jokes.punchline: The eye-roll physically hurts due to the surgery — her signature condescension reflex is literally incapacitated, which is a perfect physical-meets-character joke
jokes.explanation: This is a wonderfully specific physical comedy moment that only works because we know Selina's eye-rolling is a key character behavior. The surgery has literally suppressed her most condescending reflex. The pain yelp followed by forced acquiescence is a great comedic beat. High character integration score.

jokes.text: They yelled at Grandpa, obviously. Some truths are eternal.
jokes.setup: A senator on a news show is discussing the first Thanksgiving — the pilgrims ate deer and vegetables
jokes.punchline: 'They yelled at Grandpa, obviously. Some truths are eternal.' — the senator matter-of-factly inserts elder abuse into an idealized historical narrative, then endorses it as timeless
jokes.explanation: A quietly sharp observational joke delivered with complete earnestness by the senator. 'Some truths are eternal' as a punchline to 'they yelled at grandpa' is unexpected and dark. The deadpan framing elevates what could be a throwaway line.

jokes.text: I mean sell my sweet white ass on a street corner jonesing.
jokes.setup: The TV anchor says he's jonesing for coffee
jokes.punchline: He escalates the desperation to a graphic sexual/economic extreme — his coffee need is equivalent to prostitution-level desperation, and he describes it with cheerful specificity
jokes.explanation: A competent escalation gag. 'Sell my sweet white ass on a street corner' is a vivid if familiar hyperbole. The specificity of 'sweet white' is the only thing that distinguishes it from standard 'jonesing badly' exaggerations.

jokes.text: Also one cream, splash of half-and-half, one sugar, one Splenda. [pause] If that's not too much trouble.
jokes.setup: Tom James has asked Dan for a coffee after Dan volunteered to get one; Dan is a senior political operative who has been tacitly demoted to coffee-fetching duty
jokes.punchline: Tom James specifies an absurdly specific coffee order, then adds 'If that's not too much trouble' — the politeness making it more insulting than rudeness would
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is in the dynamic — Dan, who fancies himself a killer political operative, is fetching coffee with an absurdly specific order. Tom James' 'If that's not too much trouble' is the polite knife twist. The joke has a setup and a payoff that runs across the episode (the order repeats when Dan actually delivers it).

jokes.text: Dan realizes Tom James has been getting him to fetch coffee — he's been played into running errands.
jokes.setup: Tom James describes his elaborate coffee order while Dan agrees to get it.
jokes.punchline: Dan (and the audience) realizes he's been outmaneuvered by Tom into serving as his coffee boy — the great manipulator reduced to running café errands for someone more powerful.
jokes.explanation: Primarily a visual/situational gag — Dan's dawning realization. The elaborate coffee order (one cream, splash of half-and-half, one sugar, one Splenda) is funny in its specificity, and the fact that Dan fetches it twice in the episode makes it a running gag about his diminished status.

jokes.text: Her mouth is swollen from dental surgery. She can't make a statement. / She's not not incapacitated. She's just not fully capacitated. You fucking people. You treat the Constitution like a build-your-own-pizza menu.
jokes.setup: Ben tries to convince Doyle to make a statement in Selina's place; Doyle demands to know why the president can't do it
jokes.punchline: 'She's not not incapacitated' / 'not fully capacitated' is a double negative sophistry that's both legally absurd and linguistically funny; 'build-your-own-pizza menu' for the Constitution is an inspired and highly quotable simile
jokes.explanation: Two excellent jokes in rapid succession. 'Not not incapacitated / not fully capacitated' is a perfect bureaucratic double-speak spiral — the grammar itself becomes the comedy as Ben tries to construct a sentence that is simultaneously true and false. Then Doyle's 'build-your-own-pizza menu' for Constitutional interpretation is a genuinely great original simile — it captures selective originalism perfectly in a casual, devastating image. This exchange is among the episode's best and approaches the top anchor in quotability.

jokes.text: Sir, you're calm, you're confident, you exude a crotchety gravitas. We need to shut this down like a fucking public school for the arts.
jokes.setup: Ben is trying to convince Doyle to make a public statement
jokes.punchline: 'Crotchety gravitas' is an oxymoronic but accurate description of Doyle; 'shut this down like a fucking public school for the arts' is an absurdly specific simile for swift, merciless closure
jokes.explanation: 'Shut this down like a fucking public school for the arts' is a brilliantly original simile — arts programs in underfunded schools being closed swiftly and without ceremony is a precise and dark image for efficient crisis management. 'Crotchety gravitas' is also a sharp character observation. Ben's writing in this episode is consistently the strongest.

jokes.text: Jesus, Mike. I'm gonna put a baby in you if you don't get your head in the game.
jokes.setup: Mike is distracted during the press conference because he just got news that his surrogate is a go
jokes.punchline: Ben threatens to impregnate Mike as a punishment for inattention — the threat is biologically absurd and delivered with complete matter-of-fact menace
jokes.explanation: The joke works as a callback to Mike's baby news — Ben weaponizes the surrogacy plot as a threat. The absurdity of the threat (Ben can't put a baby in Mike) is secondary to the deadpan delivery. Works as character comedy and a callback.

jokes.text: Christ, I thought he was gonna shit the bed. Instead he's doing turndown service and leaving a mint on the pillow. You know, that can be the difference between a three- and a four-star hotel.
jokes.setup: Doyle is performing surprisingly well at the press conference after Ben thought he'd fail
jokes.punchline: Ben extends the hotel metaphor to its absurd conclusion — Doyle going from expected disaster to exceeding expectations is framed as a hotel star-rating upgrade, culminating in a deadpan endorsement of mint-on-pillow service
jokes.explanation: A strong extended metaphor. 'Shit the bed' to 'turndown service and mint on the pillow' is a clever escalation through a consistent hotel service metaphor. The extra beat about the difference between three and four stars is a good deadpan extension that commits to the metaphor rather than dropping it.

jokes.text: Is he the one with the prostitute problem? Mmm... Oh, never mind. They all have that. House is like Caligula's room.
jokes.setup: Selina is preparing to make congressional calls and is going through her list
jokes.punchline: Selina asks which congressman has the prostitute problem, then immediately realizes the question is meaningless because they all do — the House as 'Caligula's room' is an economical summary of congressional debauchery
jokes.explanation: 'They all have that. House is like Caligula's room' is a great construction — the casual sweep of 'they all have that' followed by the classical allusion to Caligula is an unexpected tonal collision. The line is fast, dark, and perfectly in Selina's voice.

jokes.text: Bordello Bill is giving me advice.
jokes.setup: Congressman Jaeger (who has a 'prostitute problem') is advising Selina on crisis management
jokes.punchline: Selina's nickname for Jaeger — 'Bordello Bill' — delivered as a quiet aside mid-phone call is a perfectly timed internal monologue joke
jokes.explanation: 'Bordello Bill' is a highly economical nickname — two words that capture the character entirely. The timing of it, whispered mid-call, is the kind of throwaway that Veep excels at. High economy score.

jokes.text: Bordello Bill is giving me advice.
jokes.setup: Congressman Jaeger (previously identified as having a prostitute problem) is advising Selina on crisis management
jokes.punchline: Selina aside-murmurs his nickname: 'Bordello Bill'
jokes.explanation: A tight callback with a memorable nickname. 'Bordello Bill' is alliterative and specific — exactly the kind of political insider nickname this show loves. Good economy and it pays off the earlier setup.

jokes.text: Coffey. Where does he stick his dick?
jokes.setup: Selina moves to the next congressman on her call list
jokes.punchline: Having just established that all congressmen have prostitute problems, Selina's first question about the next one is to inquire about his specific sexual indiscretion — the running gag of congressman corruption reaches its reductive comic peak
jokes.explanation: The running gag payoff. Having established the rule that all congressmen have sex scandals, Selina applies it immediately — her political intelligence briefing has devolved into a sex scandal rolodex. The four-word question is maximally economical.

jokes.text: Jeez, he couldn't eat a turkey unless you put it in a blender and fed it to him through a straw.
jokes.setup: They report that Congressman Sherman has been hospitalized with salmonella poisoning — he's very old
jokes.punchline: Selina's characterization of Sherman as so decrepit he'd need blended turkey through a straw — delivered with zero compassion for a hospitalized man — establishes the dark political calculus that follows
jokes.explanation: A characterful dark joke — Selina's contemptuous assessment of Sherman's advanced age while he's in the hospital is exactly the kind of political sociopathy Veep mines. The blender-and-straw image is vivid and harsh.

jokes.text: CNN and Fox both say he's critical, but Fox says it with blonder hair and bigger tits.
jokes.setup: The team is monitoring news coverage of Sherman's condition
jokes.punchline: The joke reduces Fox News' on-air identity to physical appearance ('blonder hair and bigger tits') rather than editorial content — and implies their presentation is their only distinguishing characteristic
jokes.explanation: A reliably effective media satire joke. The construction ('CNN says X, Fox says X with blonder hair and bigger tits') is a clean parallel structure. Slightly familiar territory for political comedy but executed crisply.

jokes.text: And one cream. Splash of half-and-half. One sugar and one Splenda. [long pause] Fuck me.
jokes.setup: Dan has successfully fetched Tom James his coffee after memorizing the complex order; Tom James repeats each element as if checking it off
jokes.punchline: The pause after Tom James repeats the final order element, followed by Dan's 'Fuck me' — the joke is the humiliation of Dan having to serve a precise coffee order like a barista while pretending to be a political heavyweight
jokes.explanation: The callback payoff is excellent. The humor is in the exquisite specificity of Tom James verifying each element ('And one cream. Splash of half-and-half...') like a sommelier, and Dan's dead-eyed 'Fuck me' is the perfect response — the sound of a man's pride evaporating. Structurally tight and the rewatch bonus applies because knowing Dan's arc makes this funnier.

jokes.text: Sherman's dead. / Ow! Oh, my God, that hurts so much. Please don't surprise me like that again. / Why couldn't he have waited until after the weekend?
jokes.setup: Selina gets sudden news mid-phone call that Congressman Sherman has died
jokes.punchline: Selina yelps in pain (her surgery reacting to the shock), then complains that Sherman should have timed his death more conveniently — the self-absorption is complete: her eye pain and her political inconvenience both outweigh the death
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed multi-beat joke. The physical comedy of the surgical eye reacting to surprise shock, followed by the complaint about the surprise itself, followed by the reframing of death as a scheduling inconvenience — all three beats work individually and escalate together. 'Why couldn't he have waited until after the weekend?' is a genuinely shocking line that perfectly encapsulates Selina's political sociopathy. High character integration: this could only be Selina.

jokes.text: Well, in many ways, this could be the best thing that could happen. / Say what, now?
jokes.setup: Ben pivots immediately to the political opportunity in Sherman's death
jokes.punchline: 'Say what, now?' — Selina's mock-surprise at the ghoulishness of what Ben's about to say, followed immediately by her agreeing, shows she was already there
jokes.explanation: A solid cringe beat — the 'say what now?' performs a moment of moral hesitation that neither character actually feels, a tiny ritual of pretend-conscience before diving into the opportunism. Good character work.

jokes.text: If I had known that, I would have killed him myself.
jokes.setup: Ben explains that Sherman's death helps Selina politically — one less O'Brien vote and possibly one for her
jokes.punchline: Selina says she would have murdered him personally if she'd known the electoral math — a maximally dark joke delivered as an obvious political calculation rather than a threat
jokes.explanation: A perfectly economical dark joke. Seven words that reveal Selina's complete political amorality with cheerful hypothetical murder. The matter-of-fact delivery is key — she means it purely arithmetically, which is darker than if she meant it literally. Good character integration.

jokes.text: What is dental surgery code for anyway, Ben? I mean, is it like anal bleaching? Because if it is, you should just come out and say that because the American people, they'll accept it in time.
jokes.setup: Doyle confronts Ben about the pretense that Selina had dental surgery; he clearly knows it's something else
jokes.punchline: Doyle suggests 'anal bleaching' as the thing being covered up — and then magnanimously assures that the public would eventually accept a president who bleaches their anus
jokes.explanation: Doyle's 'the American people will accept it in time' is the best part — delivered with the same world-weary certainty with which he'd discuss actual policy. It implies a fascinating theory of political tolerance that the show doesn't pursue but doesn't need to. The joke is dark, specific, and characterful: Doyle uses his political gravitas to cheerfully discuss anal bleaching acceptability.

jokes.text: To be honest with you, sir, she's not looking real good. / And I'll be honest with you. I don't think you're being honest about being honest.
jokes.setup: Ben tries to be diplomatically honest about Selina's condition to Doyle
jokes.punchline: Doyle's 'I don't think you're being honest about being honest' — a reflexive meta-honesty claim that spirals into itself. Doyle knows the layers of spin and calls them all out simultaneously
jokes.explanation: The recursive honesty-about-honesty construction is very Veep — it's the show's political cynicism expressed through grammatical self-consumption. 'Being honest about being honest' is a satisfying tongue-twister with genuine philosophical punch about political communication.

jokes.text: If she wants me to do her bullshit, she can tell me out of her own shining white asshole.
jokes.setup: Doyle insists on seeing the president directly rather than receiving messages through Ben
jokes.punchline: 'Her own shining white asshole' as the anatomical location from which Selina should deliver her requests — the 'shining white' is an unexpectedly vivid descriptor that elevates the insult from crude to absurdly specific
jokes.explanation: 'Shining white asshole' is the kind of Iannucci-ism that gets quoted — the adjectival precision ('shining white') applied to an anatomical insult is what separates Veep's profanity from generic vulgarity. Doyle's patrician gravity delivering this line makes it land harder.

jokes.text: Today's media-go-round was a tour De force. / More like a waste De time.
jokes.setup: Dan compliments Tom James' media work with the French phrase 'tour de force'
jokes.punchline: Tom James rephrases it as 'waste De time' — using the French 'de' construction to mock Dan's flattery while dismissing his own work
jokes.explanation: 'Waste De time' is a clever wordplay construction — it uses the exact structural frame of 'tour de force' to reverse the compliment. The French 'de' preservation is the key to the joke working as wordplay rather than just a dismissal. Elegant and economical.

jokes.text: Maybe I can say 'be' and also 'careful.'
jokes.setup: Dan has caught Tom James name-dropping Sidney Purcell's clients and confronts him; Tom James deflects by noting he also used common words
jokes.punchline: Tom James reduces Dan's pattern-recognition to absurdity by pointing out he also said 'be' and 'careful' — turning the evidence into nonsense through reductio ad absurdum
jokes.explanation: A tight logical joke — Tom James undermines Dan's conspiracy theory by extending the word-spotting logic to absurdity ('be' and 'careful'). It's a sharp rhetorical move that also characterizes Tom James as someone who can dismantle arguments calmly and amusingly. Good economy.

jokes.text: I will bring you back human ears for a necklace. I am your guy.
jokes.setup: Dan is trying to convince Tom James he's completely loyal and will do anything
jokes.punchline: 'Human ears for a necklace' as the demonstration of absolute loyalty — Dan's ambition expressed through imagery of war-crime trophy-taking
jokes.explanation: Dan's sociopathic loyalty pledges are a reliable character comedy vein. 'Human ears for a necklace' is vivid and dark, though slightly in the expected range for Dan's brand of excess.

jokes.text: It was not a breakdown. It was just more a little bit of psychological indigestion.
jokes.setup: Tom James brings up Dan's panic attack/nervous breakdown during the election
jokes.punchline: 'Psychological indigestion' as the euphemism for a breakdown — a self-minimizing reframe that uses a digestive metaphor to normalize a mental health crisis
jokes.explanation: 'Psychological indigestion' is a genuinely original euphemism — it miniaturizes mental collapse to a digestive inconvenience. The 'just more a little bit of' qualifier makes it even funnier by hedging the already-minimized reframe. High originality for the specific metaphor.

jokes.text: Your lips are looking a little dry. I just want to see if I have that balm.
jokes.setup: Tom James has just threatened to tell everyone Dan is 'spinning out,' neutralizing his conspiracy theory accusation; Dan is defeated
jokes.punchline: Tom James immediately pivots to offering lip balm — the total dominance of the exchange is underscored by this domestic non-sequitur, making clear the threat is over and he's moved on
jokes.explanation: The comedic principle here is the pivot — the complete deescalation of a power play into mundane concern for Dan's lips. Tom James' casual concern ('I just want to see if I have that balm') makes the domination total and the humiliation absolute. A great character beat.

jokes.text: Jesus Horatio Christ.
jokes.setup: Doyle enters to see Selina's bruised, post-surgical face
jokes.punchline: The exclamation 'Jesus Horatio Christ' — giving Jesus a middle name is an absurdist escalation of the standard blasphemy, and Doyle's specific phrasing reveals his Old Testament gravitas extends even to his profanity
jokes.explanation: 'Horatio' as Jesus's middle name is a perfect absurdist detail — it's completely unexpected, sounds vaguely naval and establishment (Horatio Nelson, etc.), and says everything about Doyle's character through a swear word. High economy and quotability.

jokes.text: Sherman may have been from the other side of the aisle, but he's a decent guy even though he still used the word 'coloreds.'
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to say kind things about the dead congressman
jokes.punchline: The qualifier 'even though he still used the word coloreds' — delivered as a minor reservation rather than a disqualifying character flaw — is a dark observation about how low the bar is in Washington
jokes.explanation: A sharp political satire joke. 'Even though he still used the word coloreds' as the minor caveat in an otherwise positive eulogy is a dark commentary on congressional tolerance of racism. The 'even though' framing rather than 'but' makes it sound almost forgiving, which is the joke.

jokes.text: 'Sherman may have been from the other side of the aisle, but he's a decent guy even though he still used the word coloreds.' 'Nobody's perfect, right?'
jokes.setup: Selina is offering condolences about the recently deceased Congressman Sherman.
jokes.punchline: She eulogizes him as 'decent' and then immediately qualifies it with his use of a racial slur, which Doyle waves away as nobody being perfect.
jokes.explanation: Dark political satire — the eulogy convention of 'he was good but' applied to casual racism, then immediately normalized by Doyle's 'nobody's perfect.' The banality of the racism being one quirk among many is the sharp point of the joke.

jokes.text: Oh, well. Nobody's perfect, right?
jokes.setup: Selina mentions that Sherman used racist language as a mild criticism
jokes.punchline: Doyle's 'Nobody's perfect' applied to documented racism is a darkly funny equivalence that the show plays completely straight — as if racism is a minor personality quirk like leaving the cap off the toothpaste
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — three words that endorse Selina's normalization of racism without even trying. Doyle's moral equivalence is delivered with patrician ease. Good callback to joke 54.

jokes.text: I will go out there with my golden boy smile. Give 'em the Doyle dazzle.
jokes.setup: Doyle agrees to make statements in Selina's place in exchange for Secretary of State consideration
jokes.punchline: 'Doyle dazzle' as self-branding — the bombastic confidence of an old politician who has given himself a nickname for his own charm — is slightly pathetic and therefore funny
jokes.explanation: Doyle using 'Doyle dazzle' is characterful — old-school politicians with alliterative personal brands. The slight self-aggrandizement is endearing and funny in context.

jokes.text: And he also said he'd kick you off the ticket. See, there's that Doyle dazzle and I'm a big fan of it.
jokes.setup: Doyle mentions that President Hughes promised him Secretary of State if he won a second term
jokes.punchline: Selina counters that Hughes also said he'd kick Doyle off the ticket — neutralizing the leverage — then immediately compliments Doyle's 'Doyle dazzle,' deflecting conflict with hollow flattery
jokes.explanation: Selina immediately weaponizes 'Doyle dazzle' back at Doyle after he introduced it — the callback lands well because the compliment is transparently manipulative. Selina's ability to pivot from devastating counter to syrupy flattery in one breath is peak character.

jokes.text: Say, did Ben do this to you? Because I can get you to a shelter. / I love it. Domestic abuse jokes. They just tickle me.
jokes.setup: Doyle looks at Selina's bruised face post-surgery
jokes.punchline: Doyle jokes that Ben beat her and offers shelter resources, then Selina's reply — 'I love it. Domestic abuse jokes. They just tickle me' — is an ironic endorsement that is itself a joke about the culture of political masculine dominance
jokes.explanation: The two-part exchange works — Doyle's shelter offer is darkly funny, and Selina's ironic response ('they just tickle me') acknowledges the darkness while playing it as political insider banter. Slightly uneven in terms of the 'earned vs cheap' axis given domestic abuse as subject matter, but in Veep's universe of casual cruelty it fits.

jokes.text: I think Tom is up to something with Sidney Purcell... And he said that I was crazy. / You know what it could be? That you are fucking crazy. / Tom James. / It was a fucking panic attack. / Tom James. / Goddamn panic attack.
jokes.setup: Dan presents his conspiracy theory about Tom James to Amy and Kent
jokes.punchline: Kent dismisses Dan with 'Tom James' twice — a two-word rebuttal that treats Dan's conspiracy theory as evidence of his own unstable mental state; the repetition with Dan's escalating insistence ('it was a fucking panic attack') makes it funnier each time
jokes.explanation: This sequence is a masterclass in comedic rhythm. Kent's repetition of 'Tom James' as a complete rebuttal — twice — is structurally perfect. By simply saying the name of the person Dan is accusing, Kent implies that the name alone is a sufficient refutation of any conspiracy theory. Dan's 'Goddamn panic attack' being ignored entirely by Kent is the escalation that makes it. High structure and economy scores.

jokes.text: Listen, baby, I have some giblets simmering and five really hungry people, so I'm gonna need you to be really quick. / No problem. Fastest gun in DC.
jokes.setup: Mike calls home on Thanksgiving having abandoned his family to work
jokes.punchline: Wendy's 'giblets simmering' creates a warm domestic image that makes Mike's absence crueler; 'Fastest gun in DC' is Mike's self-aggrandizing response to being told to hurry — ironic given that he's clearly not in control of anything
jokes.explanation: Competent character comedy — Mike's bravado ('Fastest gun in DC') while failing to be present for his family is on-brand. The joke is functional but not outstanding.

jokes.text: Do you smell something? No, nothing. Just the whiff of romance.
jokes.setup: Mike has snuck into a hotel room for a 'surprise' afternoon quickie with his wife to celebrate surrogate news
jokes.punchline: The wife smells something wrong in the hotel room while Mike cheerfully attributes any smell to 'romance' — the irony being that Mike has booked a questionable hotel room where romance is the last thing it smells like
jokes.explanation: A setup for the upcoming visual punchline (the hotel room smell). Functional situational comedy but the setup-without-full-payoff makes it feel incomplete in transcript form.

jokes.text: What is this Tom James guy like? Is he tall? / I should get back to work. The president needs me.
jokes.setup: Amy is at her parents' home for Thanksgiving; her father asks about Tom James
jokes.punchline: Amy's instant and desperate attempt to escape the conversation by invoking presidential duty — the president as an alibi from family interaction
jokes.explanation: A small character beat — Amy's discomfort with family is well-established. The joke is in the speed of her retreat. Functional but minor.

jokes.text: Well, one of us did, but what happens in Vegas stays in Vegas. / Jesus, for the final time, it was not Vegas!
jokes.setup: Amy's dad asks about her trip (Nevada recount), calling it 'Vegas'
jokes.punchline: Amy's explosive correction reveals this is an ongoing frustration — her father keeps misremembering or deliberately mislabeling the catastrophic recount as a Vegas trip, trivializing her professional humiliation
jokes.explanation: A solid family cringe joke — Dad's cheerful misremembering of Amy's professional disaster as a fun trip is a perfect parental obliviousness gag. Amy's scream ('it was NOT Vegas') captures the exhaustion of explaining your life to family.

jokes.text: Amy's father asks about Tom James and Amy says 'I should get back to work' — implying something happened in Vegas.
jokes.setup: Amy's dad asks about Tom James; Amy dodges.
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'I should get back to work' and the implication from her dad ('what happens in Vegas stays in Vegas') suggests Amy had a personal encounter with Tom James.
jokes.explanation: A softer joke — the comedy is in what's not said. Amy's extreme discomfort and her dad's cheerful 'what happens in Vegas' implication builds the character dynamic. Rewatch bonus for the subplot payoff.

jokes.text: I fully support your decision to live as an ugly woman.
jokes.setup: Dan appears at Amy's family home unexpectedly; Amy opens the door
jokes.punchline: Dan frames Amy's appearance (presumably dressed down at home with family) as a 'decision to live as an ugly woman' — the 'support' framing is borrowed from progressive identity language and applied as a vicious insult
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'I fully support your decision to live as an ugly woman' weaponizes the vocabulary of progressive tolerance as an insult — the 'decision to live as' framing is borrowed from gender identity discourse and twisted into cruelty. It's shocking, original, and perfectly Dan: his insults always have a structural sophistication that separates them from generic mean-girl put-downs. High on all craft dimensions.

jokes.text: Sounds like a big goddamn but. / Oh, yeah, this is a giant, juicy, muscular Serena Williams but.
jokes.setup: Jeff is about to deliver bad news about the New Hampshire special election
jokes.punchline: 'A giant, juicy, muscular Serena Williams but' — an original and vivid simile for a massive qualification, name-dropping Serena Williams' physique as the standard for a powerful 'but'
jokes.explanation: 'Giant, juicy, muscular Serena Williams but' is a genuinely original simile. The specificity of Serena Williams as the benchmark for an impressive 'but' (homophone of butt making it double) is unexpected and vivid. The three adjectives 'giant, juicy, muscular' applied to a conjunction before a celebrity name is a great Veep construction.

jokes.text: You don't give me names, Beardo. I give you names. So take your list, roll it up real tiny, attach it to the leg of a carrier pigeon, and have it fly up tubby's dick.
jokes.setup: Jeff refuses Ben's suggestion to work from a provided list of candidate names
jokes.punchline: Jeff constructs an elaborate, specific instruction for how Ben should dispose of the list — involving a carrier pigeon and an unspecified 'tubby' — the escalating specificity of the disposal method is funnier than any single element
jokes.explanation: The carrier pigeon construction is a great escalation joke — Jeff doesn't just say 'shove it,' he builds an elaborate and specific alternative communication system specifically so the list can end up in someone's penis. The 'roll it up real tiny' step is the key detail that sells the absurdist commitment. One of Jeff's best lines and a strong contender for episode highlight.

jokes.text: I have recurring nightmares about running against widows.
jokes.setup: Sherman's widow is running for his seat
jokes.punchline: Jeff's casual confession of widow-specific political PTSD — the specificity of 'recurring nightmares' about widows (not general opponents) implies a rich history of widow-related defeats
jokes.explanation: A perfectly economical character joke. 'Recurring nightmares about widows' implies Jeff's entire political career has been haunted by this specific opponent type. The casualness with which he confesses it — as if it's an obvious polling consideration — is very funny.

jokes.text: Brilliant, handsome, wife's a solid eight. After kids, probably still be a seven.
jokes.setup: Jeff describes his nephew Ezra, who he's grooming for the seat
jokes.punchline: Jeff rates Ezra's wife as a numerical score and projects her future attractiveness post-childbirth — reducing a person to a political asset metric with the dispassion of a cattle auctioneer
jokes.explanation: The casual projective 'after kids, probably still be a seven' is the key comedic element — Jeff is so comfortable treating human beings as political assets that he accounts for physical depreciation over time as part of his electoral calculus. Dark and very character-specific.

jokes.text: Besides, for anyone to beat the widow, they're gonna have to fight dirty and I don't shit where I eat. / Well, me neither, not since my wife caught me eating yogurt on the crapper.
jokes.setup: Jeff says he won't fight dirty against the widow because he doesn't 'shit where he eats'
jokes.punchline: Ben takes the idiom literally and confesses to eating yogurt on the toilet — a TMI domestic admission that completely derails the political conversation
jokes.explanation: A great misdirection/literal-idiom joke. Jeff uses 'shit where I eat' idiomatically; Ben takes it literally with the specific image of yogurt on a crapper and a wife who witnessed it. The specificity of 'yogurt' (as opposed to any other food) is a perfect Veep detail — it's banal enough to be pathetically realistic. 'My wife caught me' implies a history of such behavior. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: We need a real piece of cannon fodder here, some spectacular dumbass willing to charge this machine gun nest, sacrifice his name and reputation, and then fuck off so Ezra can slide right in.
jokes.setup: Jeff needs someone to run against the widow Sherman to keep the seat warm for Ezra
jokes.punchline: Jeff's description of his ideal candidate — 'spectacular dumbass' who will 'sacrifice his name and reputation and then fuck off' — is a devastatingly honest job description that turns political cynicism into dark comedy
jokes.explanation: 'Spectacular dumbass' is a great oxymoron — the dumbassery must be of high quality. The full job description of 'charge the machine gun nest, sacrifice his name, then fuck off' is a perfect dark articulation of how political kingmakers actually think.

jokes.text: There's only one name on it. [cut to Jonah at the Ryan family Thanksgiving]
jokes.setup: Jeff asks if they have a list of 'spectacular dumbasses'
jokes.punchline: The cut to Jonah is the punchline — we don't need to be told who the spectacular dumbass is; the visual cut is the joke
jokes.explanation: The scene cut IS the punchline — the 'spectacular dumbass' is revealed to be Jonah by juxtaposition alone. This is pure cinematic joke craft: 'there's only one name on it' followed by an immediate cut to Jonah eating Thanksgiving dinner with his family is perfectly structured. The economy of using the edit rather than stating the name is exceptional. High structure score because the joke lives in the form of the storytelling, not just the content.

jokes.text: For my family back in Connecticut, we ditched the turkey. That's because I have a special recipe for farmed salmon with a side of zucchini.
jokes.setup: Tom James is on a TV cooking segment; Amy and Dan are watching, trying to decode his Sidney Purcell connection
jokes.punchline: Tom James mentions farmed salmon AND zucchini in the same breath — both Purcell clients mentioned earlier in the episode — the callback confirms Dan's theory while Tom James remains plausibly deniable
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff on the Tom James/Purcell subplot. The comedy is in the simultaneous confirmation (he's doing it again) and deflation (so what?) — Dan's correct but it doesn't matter because no one cares. The rewatch bonus applies strongly: on rewatch you hear the subplot all through the episode and the full comedy of Tom James' blatant product placement lands.

jokes.text: So for dessert, there's apple and pecan pie. What would you like? / You know, I would love a little bit of both. / Oh, you want both, do you? / Yeah, why not? / I'll tell you why not, you shit sack Casanova. You have had sex with both of my daughters.
jokes.setup: Dan is at Thanksgiving with Amy's family; the dad offers pie choices
jokes.punchline: Dad's escalation from a pie conversation to revealing Dan has slept with both of his daughters — the domestic mildness of 'apple and pecan pie' as the trigger for this revelation is perfect misdirection
jokes.explanation: The episode's best pure joke. The setup is impeccable: a completely innocuous pie selection question, Dan's equally innocuous answer ('a little bit of both'), the dad's pause before applying Dan's answer to his daughters, and the reveal that Dan has slept with both daughters — 'shit sack Casanova' as the epithet is a wonderful compound insult. The misdirection is elegant, the escalation is perfect, and 'you want both, do you?' reframes Dan's pie answer in real time. This approaches the 9+ anchor in structure and impact.

jokes.text: Wait a minute, sweetie, I just figured it out. You know what it is? You want to fuck my wife, too, don't you?
jokes.setup: Dad has just confronted Dan about sleeping with both daughters
jokes.punchline: Dad's mid-sentence realization that Dan's 'both' logic must extend to his wife — the escalation from daughters to wife is a logical extrapolation that reveals Dad is thinking through Dan's predatory pattern in real time
jokes.explanation: The escalation of joke 75 — having discovered Dan slept with both daughters, Dad's mind immediately applies the 'want both' logic to include his wife. 'I just figured it out' implies he's been thinking about this during the previous beat. The mid-realization 'wait a minute, sweetie' directed at Amy is also a nice touch — he's involving her as he processes the revelation.

jokes.text: Mrs. Ryan, these old Thanksgiving photos are priceless. Wow, that is some overbite. It's like a wererabbit.
jokes.setup: Richard is looking at old Thanksgiving photos at the Ryan family home
jokes.punchline: 'Wererabbit' as the descriptor for Jonah's childhood overbite — a specific, absurdist coinage that's funnier than any existing word would be
jokes.explanation: 'Wererabbit' is a delightful coinage — blending werewolf mythology with rabbit (for overbite/buck teeth) is unexpected and specific. Richard delivers it with complete earnestness, which is his character comedy brand. The word itself is the joke and it's a good one.

jokes.text: Well, I was advanced for my age. I had all my adult teeth by age four.
jokes.setup: Richard has compared young Jonah's overbite to a wererabbit
jokes.punchline: Mrs. Ryan defends young Jonah's grotesque dental situation as a sign of advancement — the gap between how she frames it ('advanced') and what it means (having a monstrous overbite very young) is the joke
jokes.explanation: The maternal spin on the wererabbit observation is a good character beat — Mom defensively reframes a deformity as precocity. 'All my adult teeth by age four' as evidence of giftedness is darkly funny parental logic.

jokes.text: I didn't know children still wore leashes at this age.
jokes.setup: Richard is still looking at old Ryan family Thanksgiving photos; another photo shows young Jonah on a child leash
jokes.punchline: Richard's observation that the leash is unusual for Jonah's apparent age — delivered with genuine puzzlement rather than as an insult — is funnier for being sincere
jokes.explanation: Richard's earnest confusion about the leash — he doesn't know Jonah's age in the photo, implying the leash is age-inappropriate — is pure Richard character comedy. His inability to recognize when something is an insult makes his observations funnier.

jokes.text: No, I want you to run. / You want me to run his campaign? / No, Jonah, I want you to run for the seat.
jokes.setup: Jeff tells Jonah about the congressional seat and that he should run
jokes.punchline: Jonah immediately assumes Jeff means run someone else's campaign — the triple misdirection (run → run his campaign → run for the seat) mirrors Jonah's inability to conceive of himself as a principal rather than a functionary, which is itself funny
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection/misunderstanding joke that's perfectly character-integrated — Jonah's assumption that he'd be running someone else's campaign reveals both his professional self-image and his genuine shock at being considered a candidate. The three-beat exchange is tight.

jokes.text: Dear Lord who guides me and nourishes me, I set foot on this path that you have laid before me with a strong arm and a willing heart to totally rock this shit. Amen.
jokes.setup: Jonah says grace/a prayer before his congressional announcement
jokes.punchline: 'To totally rock this shit. Amen.' — the shift from formal prayer language ('strong arm and a willing heart') to Jonah's natural register ('totally rock this shit') is a perfect tonal collision
jokes.explanation: Jonah's prayer is a perfect character comedy set piece. The structure — sustained formal biblical register — building to 'to totally rock this shit. Amen.' — is textbook tonal pivot. The 'amen' landing after 'shit' is the final note that makes it. Could ONLY be Jonah: the self-delusion, the grandeur, the garbled biblical rhetoric, and the bro-speak all coexist in him uniquely.

jokes.text: The Jonah Ryan story, chapter five. The House kneels before the fucking J-man. I'm running for Congress!
jokes.setup: Jonah has just decided to run for Congress
jokes.punchline: Jonah's self-mythologizing — 'chapter five,' 'The House kneels before the fucking J-man' — is a magnificent display of grandiosity from someone who has never succeeded at anything
jokes.explanation: 'Chapter five' of 'The Jonah Ryan story' — as if he's been writing his own heroic biography; 'The House kneels before the fucking J-man' — the grandiosity is catastrophically mismatched with Jonah's actual record. Character integration score is near-maximum because this is Jonah's essence.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, that's great. Actual Congress or some fantasy league Congress?
jokes.setup: Jonah announces he's running for Congress
jokes.punchline: Richard's instant follow-up question — 'actual Congress or some fantasy league Congress' — implies he's so unimpressed by Jonah that fantasy Congress seems equally plausible
jokes.explanation: 'Actual Congress or some fantasy league Congress?' is a great line — 'fantasy league Congress' is a perfect original coinage that implies Jonah's ambitions are at the level of a sports nerd's hobby. Richard's genuine uncertainty between the two options is the character comedy. High quotability because the line has real standalone life.

jokes.text: Of course, up against the Widow Sherman, it's gonna be like climbing Mt. McKinley with your balls stapled to a piano.
jokes.setup: Jonah is celebrating his congressional run
jokes.punchline: 'Climbing Mt. McKinley with your balls stapled to a piano' — a gloriously specific and painful image for an impossible task that combines extreme physical exertion with extreme physical torture
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines and one of the best pure similes in Veep's run. 'Climbing Mt. McKinley with your balls stapled to a piano' is extraordinary — it combines the epic (North America's highest peak) with the grotesque (scrotal stapling) with the absurd (a piano as the instrument of torture). The specificity of Mt. McKinley over Everest is the right choice — it's distinctly American, just hard enough. This approaches the top craft anchor in originality and quotability.

jokes.text: She's got my vote, bless her heart.
jokes.setup: Jeff has noted that Jonah running against the widow will be nearly impossible
jokes.punchline: Jonah's own mother immediately announces she'd vote for the widow against her son — 'bless her heart' adding the Southern passive-aggressive register to the maternal betrayal
jokes.explanation: A devastating character joke. Jonah's mother would vote for the widow over him, and her 'bless her heart' makes it sound sympathetic while being savage. The joke tells us everything about both Jonah's family relationships and his actual public standing. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Ain't a challenge been invented Jonah Ryan can't do. / Ice bucket challenge. You backed out of that. / Ice bucket challenge can suck my dick. / Well, it did raise a lot of awareness for whatever ALS is.
jokes.setup: Jonah claims he can overcome any challenge
jokes.punchline: Richard immediately cites the ice bucket challenge as a counterexample; Jonah's dismissal ('can suck my dick'); Richard's follow-up noting the charity's success — 'for whatever ALS is' — delivered with genuine ignorance
jokes.explanation: 'For whatever ALS is' is a brilliant Richard line — his sincere ignorance of what ALS is while acknowledging the awareness campaign worked is perfect character comedy. The four-beat exchange is tight: boast, deflation, dismissal, earnest add-on. 'Ice bucket challenge can suck my dick' is peak Jonah. The sequence builds well and Richard's final line is the highlight — it could only come from Richard.

jokes.text: Who is this man? / Richard Splett. / Splett? / Splett.
jokes.setup: Jeff doesn't know who Richard is
jokes.punchline: The exchange of 'Splett? / Splett.' — the name itself is funny (monosyllabic, wet-sounding, ridiculous) and the confirmation as if the name explains everything is a great economy joke
jokes.explanation: The joke is the name and the echo. 'Splett?' as a question, 'Splett.' as a confirmation — as if the name is both ludicrous and self-explanatory. The phonetics of 'Splett' do a lot of work. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: I went to the White House on a three-week placement. I'm a MRSA infection. You don't get rid of Jonah Ryan.
jokes.setup: Jeff tells Jonah this is a one-term placeholder role
jokes.punchline: 'I'm a MRSA infection. You don't get rid of Jonah Ryan.' — Jonah compares himself to an antibiotic-resistant hospital superbug as a point of pride, which is both accurate and grotesque
jokes.explanation: An exceptional character joke. Jonah identifying as a MRSA infection is the most accurate possible metaphor for his presence in Washington — he has spread, caused misery, and cannot be eliminated by normal means. The fact that Jonah says this with pride, as a boast, is the additional layer that makes it brilliant. Could ONLY be Jonah because the metaphor perfectly captures his tenacious, toxic presence. One of the episode's very best.

jokes.text: Now listen to me, you walking trisomy. I could get dog shit in a condom elected in New Hampshire.
jokes.setup: Jeff is asserting his power over Jonah and dismissing his political relevance
jokes.punchline: 'Dog shit in a condom' as the baseline electability standard for New Hampshire — implying the state's voters are so easily manipulated that literally anything Jeff puts up will win; 'walking trisomy' as the insult for Jonah (a chromosomal abnormality reference)
jokes.explanation: 'Dog shit in a condom' is an original and vivid compound — the condom specification is the key detail that makes it funny rather than just crude (it implies a level of packaging and presentation of dog shit). 'Walking trisomy' is a medically specific insult that's fresh and dark. Both phrases are exceptional Veep insult craft. Jeff's sustained dominance of this scene through pure verbal viciousness is a character highlight.

jokes.text: You are my puppet. I'd let you dance. And when I stuff you back in the toy box to let Ezra lead, you will be grateful I ever let your wooden painted face take the stage.
jokes.setup: Jeff is explaining to Jonah that he has no real power — he's a puppet for Ezra
jokes.punchline: The sustained puppet metaphor — 'I'd let you dance,' 'stuff you back in the toy box,' 'wooden painted face' — is an extended metaphor that commits to the image fully, making Jonah's status as an object rather than a person complete
jokes.explanation: The extended puppet metaphor is well-executed — 'stuff you back in the toy box' and 'wooden painted face' are the strongest elements. The sustained commitment to the conceit rather than just invoking it once is good writing. 'I'd let you dance' has a particular menace.

jokes.text: Jeff. Jeff. / Hey, leave it. / I don't want you screaming at Joni. / Congratulations, sir. May I be the first to join your campaign? / Absolutely, chief of staff. / Please let me earn it. / There will be a vetting process.
jokes.setup: The chaos after Jeff's aggressive speech to Jonah; Richard immediately tries to join the campaign
jokes.punchline: Jonah appoints Richard chief of staff immediately; Richard's 'please let me earn it' / Jonah's 'there will be a vetting process' — the vetting process lasts zero seconds, immediately contradicting itself
jokes.explanation: The Richard/Jonah exchange is the highlight — Jonah grants him chief of staff, Richard wants to earn it through proper process, Jonah invokes 'vetting,' and this is a joke about how Jonah's campaign is already running with no standards whatsoever. Very character-integrated.

jokes.text: Oh, hey, is it possible to get salmonella if you were to make love in a turkey's bed? / I'm sorry? / I should add the turkeys were no longer there.
jokes.setup: Mike approaches the press team with a question after the Thanksgiving ceremony
jokes.punchline: Mike reveals he and his wife had sex in the pardoned turkeys' hotel room — and his follow-up 'the turkeys were no longer there' treats the turkey occupancy as the primary concern rather than the sex itself
jokes.explanation: The episode's payoff callback to the turkey hotel room — Mike has used the pardoned turkeys' hotel suite for a sex romp. 'Is it possible to get salmonella if you were to make love in a turkey's bed?' is a genuinely original and absurd question. The follow-up 'I should add the turkeys were no longer there' — offered as clarification suggesting the turkeys' presence would be worse — is perfect Mike character comedy. The episode has been building to this without telegraphing it. One of the episode's very best moments in terms of structure.

jokes.text: 'Oh, hey, is it possible to get salmonella if you were to make love in a turkey's bed? I should add the turkeys were no longer there.'
jokes.setup: Mike is at the salmonella press conference. We know he booked a hotel room for a tryst.
jokes.punchline: He approaches the CDC official with a very specific personal health question — they used the turkey suite. His clarification ('the turkeys were no longer there') somehow makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: A top-tier callback payoff. The episode has run two parallel tracks — the turkey pardon (turkeys get a hotel room) and Mike's tryst (needs a hotel room for a quickie) — and the punchline is that Mike and Wendy used the turkey hotel room. 'I should add the turkeys were no longer there' as a clarification that provides zero reassurance is perfect. The medical/sexual question to a CDC official in the middle of a salmonella briefing is impeccable timing. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: Somewhere in the world there's a woman exactly my age getting her pussy eaten and I'm stuck here watching this.
jokes.setup: Selina watches the press conference on TV, frustrated that she can't make a statement herself
jokes.punchline: Selina's specific complaint — not that someone is having fun, but that there is a woman her exact age getting oral sex while she's managing a crisis — the specificity of 'exactly my age' is the key comedic detail
jokes.explanation: 'Exactly my age' is the brilliant precision that makes this joke — Selina isn't envying youth or general pleasure, she's performing a very specific age-cohort comparison. It reveals her simultaneous vanity, loneliness, and frustrated sexuality in one remarkable sentence. Could only be Selina and only works because Julia Louis-Dreyfus's specific brand of presidential desperation is baked into the line. A standout Selina moment.

jokes.text: When Admiral Burger first suggested this trip, it seemed like a very small sacrifice to forgo a traditional Thanksgiving with my beloved daughter Catherine...
jokes.setup: Selina makes a statement at a military base about giving up Thanksgiving with Catherine to be with troops
jokes.punchline: The irony is total — we know Selina wasn't going to see Catherine anyway (she turned down the invitation explicitly), so her 'sacrifice' is entirely fictional, performed for cameras
jokes.explanation: A good structural irony payoff — the 'sacrifice' was never a sacrifice. The comedy is in the gap between Selina's speech and what we know, which is pure dramatic irony. Not a quotable joke but a satisfying structural one.

jokes.text: I also want to take this opportunity to introduce Congressman Bill Jaeger from the first district in the great state of Wisconsin. [Jaeger waves]
jokes.setup: Selina introduces Congressman Jaeger at the military base event
jokes.punchline: This is 'Bordello Bill' — the congressman with the 'prostitute problem' — being presented as a wholesome dignitary at a military Thanksgiving. The contrast between his private reputation and his public introduction is the joke
jokes.explanation: A callback that works because we know Jaeger's reputation. The visual of 'Bordello Bill' being introduced as a wholesome representative is the joke, largely delivered through dramatic irony with audience knowledge.

jokes.text: How was your Thanksgiving, Marjorie? / It was nice, ma'am. / Where were you? / Maryland, ma'am. / Oh, that's where I'm from. / I know, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Selina engages Marjorie in small talk — the exact same exchange as earlier in the episode
jokes.punchline: This is a verbatim repeat of the episode's earlier Selina/Marjorie exchange — Selina has asked and forgotten, or she just repeats her small talk patterns without registering the responses
jokes.explanation: The verbatim repeat of the Marjorie exchange is one of the episode's best structural jokes. The first time it plays as a character observation about Selina's hollow small talk; the second time it plays as a devastating revelation that Selina literally did not register the previous conversation at all. Marjorie's 'I know, ma'am' lands even harder the second time — she knew this would happen. This is textbook callback comedy: the repeat earns far more than the original. Perfect structure and economy.

jokes.text: That's tofurkey. It's made from... / Wheat, protein, organic tofu. / ...wheat, protein, and organic tofu.
jokes.setup: Selina is serving tofurkey to troops and begins to explain what it is
jokes.punchline: An aide whispers the answer mid-sentence; Selina repeats it verbatim without missing a beat — the casual ventriloquism of a president who doesn't know what tofurkey is but performs confidence anyway
jokes.explanation: A perfect Selina competence gag — she has no idea what tofurkey is but smoothly incorporates the aide's whisper into her explanation as if she knew all along. The word-for-word repetition with no acknowledgment of where the information came from is the joke. High character integration.

jokes.text: We're gonna have a mutiny. We're gonna have a mut... / We're gonna have a mutiny. Let's get some meat on your bones. Or we'll put this on your bones.
jokes.setup: The troops are receiving tofurkey instead of real turkey — Selina senses dissatisfaction
jokes.punchline: Selina's abandoned joke ('mutiny') which she starts, loses track of, restarts, and then pivots to a very odd 'put this on your bones' — the failed joke attempt is the joke
jokes.explanation: Selina losing her thread mid-joke in front of troops is a good cringe beat — the half-pivot to 'or we'll put this on your bones' is the kind of desperate improvisation she makes. Functional character comedy.

jokes.text: What? / What is that? / Oh, my God. Ah! / Oh. / Here. / Just... shit. / I bet you there's about 20 pieces of corn in here. Could be more. Probably fit a lot of corn in there. Oh, my God.
jokes.setup: Selina is serving food to troops; something goes wrong with the serving apparatus or her clothing
jokes.punchline: An unexpected corn incident — the exclamations suggest corn has been scattered or discovered somewhere it shouldn't be (her sunglasses, clothing, or the serving setup), culminating in the aide speculating about corn capacity
jokes.explanation: This is a visual/physical comedy scene that loses a lot in transcript form. The comedy appears to be slapstick (corn going somewhere unexpected) with the aide's 'probably fit a lot of corn in there' as the verbal punchline. Rewatch bonus because the visual execution likely makes this funnier than the transcript suggests. Scoring conservatively given transcript limitations.

tmdb_overview: Selina celebrates Thanksgiving in her own special way. Meanwhile, Ben and Kent manage a Thanksgiving Day crisis, deploying Tom James and VP Doyle to make statements in Selina's absence. Ben sends Dan to work for Tom James.
insight: Thanksgiving chaos spreads 72 jokes thin at 1.88-per-minute pace.
